narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and a middle-class family background, you've clearly put in significant effort to build a strong foundation for law school. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 49.3% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. This decision hinged mostly on your academic rankings from earlier years, particularly your position in the 2nd decile for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades at your school—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile spots where most successful students land. Your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signaled to the model a potential struggle with sustained high performance, even more than your Year 1 ranking. On a brighter note, your above-average undergrad GPA was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward success, showing the model you have real academic potential. Your LSAT score of 31, while below the usual 37 average, played a smaller role in favoring passage, as did your plan to attend full-time like over 90% of students and your middle-income bracket. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your early decile rankings stand out as the key shortfall, outweighing your strengths in GPA and other areas. It's tough to hear this, especially when you've achieved a strong undergrad record, but the model prioritizes patterns from past students to forecast bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, seeking ways to improve your school rankings through advanced courses or tutoring if possible, and applying to programs with more flexible admissions criteria. You might also gather letters highlighting your resilience and non-academic strengths, as humans reviewing appeals often see beyond these metrics. Keep pushing—you've got a GPA that proves you're capable, and with targeted improvements, stronger opportunities await. We're rooting for your success.
